    Dependencies in ASP.NET Core Interview Questions

    This guide covers practical dependency injection and service-composition design in ASP.NET Core. It follows the corrected format of 100 interview questions for each subtopic, and every answer includes a C# code example with rotated real-world scenarios so the examples do not repeat verbatim.

    How To Use This Page

    • Questions 1-100 cover Dependency injection container.
    • Questions 101-200 cover Service registration.
    • Questions 201-300 cover Service lifetimes.
    • Questions 301-400 cover Constructor injection.
    • Questions 401-500 cover Interface-based design.
    • Questions 501-600 cover Options pattern.
    • Questions 601-700 cover Scoped services in web requests.
    • Questions 701-800 cover Testing and mocking.
    • Questions 801-900 cover Anti-patterns.
    • Questions 901-1000 cover Troubleshooting resolution issues.

    1. Dependency injection container

    This section contains 100 interview questions focused on Dependency injection container. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

    Q1.1 What is built in DI container role in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Built in di container role means ASP.NET Core includes a built-in container that creates services and resolves dependency graphs at runtime. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual new chains everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring the container as the application composition mechanism. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderService, OrderService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IOrderService { }
        class OrderService : IOrderService { }
    }
}

    Q1.2 How does composition root concept in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Composition root concept means service wiring belongs in the startup composition root where application dependencies are declared intentionally. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with registration scattered through feature code, and they should avoid the trap of losing control of how the graph is assembled. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<IClock, SystemClock>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
    }
}

    Q1.3 Why does container versus object creation by hand in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Container versus object creation by hand means the container helps centralize creation rules lifetimes and replacement seams. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual creation in every layer, and they should avoid the trap of duplicating construction logic across the app. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new InvoiceService(new SystemClock());
        Console.WriteLine(service is not null);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
        class InvoiceService { public InvoiceService(IClock clock) { } }
    }
}

    Q1.4 When should you use container limits and responsibility in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Container limits and responsibility means the built-in container is meant for straightforward composition rather than arbitrary runtime service location. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with container as a magic toolbox, and they should avoid the trap of using it as a replacement for design discipline. Example: during a controller refactor, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool containerIsMagic = false;
        Console.WriteLine(containerIsMagic);
    }
}

    Q1.5 What problem does dependency graph resolution flow in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Dependency graph resolution flow means service resolution follows constructor dependencies recursively based on registrations and lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with resolution as unexplained magic, and they should avoid the trap of debugging graphs without understanding the dependency chain. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a controller refactor, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class Handler { public Handler(IClock clock) { } }
        interface IClock { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(Handler).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}

    Q1.6 How would you explain container interview framing in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Container interview framing means strong answers connect the container to startup composition, lifetimes, and maintainability. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with terminology only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime behavior. Example: during a web request scope investigation, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_6
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("The composition root controls how services are wired");
    }
}

    Q1.7 Why is built in DI container role in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Built in di container role means ASP.NET Core includes a built-in container that creates services and resolves dependency graphs at runtime. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual new chains everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring the container as the application composition mechanism. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_7
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderService, OrderService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IOrderService { }
        class OrderService : IOrderService { }
    }
}

    Q1.8 How can composition root concept in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Composition root concept means service wiring belongs in the startup composition root where application dependencies are declared intentionally. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with registration scattered through feature code, and they should avoid the trap of losing control of how the graph is assembled. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_8
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<IClock, SystemClock>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
    }
}

    Q1.9 What is container versus object creation by hand in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Container versus object creation by hand means the container helps centralize creation rules lifetimes and replacement seams. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual creation in every layer, and they should avoid the trap of duplicating construction logic across the app. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_9
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new InvoiceService(new SystemClock());
        Console.WriteLine(service is not null);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
        class InvoiceService { public InvoiceService(IClock clock) { } }
    }
}

    Q1.10 How does container limits and responsibility in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Container limits and responsibility means the built-in container is meant for straightforward composition rather than arbitrary runtime service location. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with container as a magic toolbox, and they should avoid the trap of using it as a replacement for design discipline. Example: during a service-registration review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_10
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool containerIsMagic = false;
        Console.WriteLine(containerIsMagic);
    }
}

    Q1.11 Why does dependency graph resolution flow in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Dependency graph resolution flow means service resolution follows constructor dependencies recursively based on registrations and lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with resolution as unexplained magic, and they should avoid the trap of debugging graphs without understanding the dependency chain. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_11
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class Handler { public Handler(IClock clock) { } }
        interface IClock { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(Handler).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}

    Q1.12 When should you use container interview framing in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Container interview framing means strong answers connect the container to startup composition, lifetimes, and maintainability. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with terminology only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime behavior. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_12
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("The composition root controls how services are wired");
    }
}

    Q1.13 What problem does built in DI container role in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Built in di container role means ASP.NET Core includes a built-in container that creates services and resolves dependency graphs at runtime. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual new chains everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring the container as the application composition mechanism. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_13
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderService, OrderService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IOrderService { }
        class OrderService : IOrderService { }
    }
}

    Q1.39 Why is container versus object creation by hand in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Container versus object creation by hand means the container helps centralize creation rules lifetimes and replacement seams. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual creation in every layer, and they should avoid the trap of duplicating construction logic across the app. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_39
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new InvoiceService(new SystemClock());
        Console.WriteLine(service is not null);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
        class InvoiceService { public InvoiceService(IClock clock) { } }
    }
}

    Q1.40 How can container limits and responsibility in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Container limits and responsibility means the built-in container is meant for straightforward composition rather than arbitrary runtime service location. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with container as a magic toolbox, and they should avoid the trap of using it as a replacement for design discipline. Example: during a service-registration review, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_40
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool containerIsMagic = false;
        Console.WriteLine(containerIsMagic);
    }
}

    Q1.41 What is dependency graph resolution flow in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Dependency graph resolution flow means service resolution follows constructor dependencies recursively based on registrations and lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with resolution as unexplained magic, and they should avoid the trap of debugging graphs without understanding the dependency chain. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_41
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class Handler { public Handler(IClock clock) { } }
        interface IClock { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(Handler).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}

    Q1.42 How does container interview framing in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Container interview framing means strong answers connect the container to startup composition, lifetimes, and maintainability. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with terminology only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime behavior. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;

{

    {
        Console.WriteLine("The composition root controls how services are wired");
    }

    Q1.43 Why does built in DI container role in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Built in di container role means ASP.NET Core includes a built-in container that creates services and resolves dependency graphs at runtime. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual new chains everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring the container as the application composition mechanism. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_43
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderService, OrderService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IOrderService { }
        class OrderService : IOrderService { }
    }
}

    Q1.44 When should you use composition root concept in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Composition root concept means service wiring belongs in the startup composition root where application dependencies are declared intentionally. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with registration scattered through feature code, and they should avoid the trap of losing control of how the graph is assembled. Example: during a controller refactor, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<IClock, SystemClock>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
    }

        class InvoiceService { public InvoiceService(IClock clock) { } }
    }
}

    Q1.70 How would you explain container limits and responsibility in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Container limits and responsibility means the built-in container is meant for straightforward composition rather than arbitrary runtime service location. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with container as a magic toolbox, and they should avoid the trap of using it as a replacement for design discipline. Example: during a service-registration review, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_70
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool containerIsMagic = false;
        Console.WriteLine(containerIsMagic);
    }
}

    Q1.71 Why is dependency graph resolution flow in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Dependency graph resolution flow means service resolution follows constructor dependencies recursively based on registrations and lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with resolution as unexplained magic, and they should avoid the trap of debugging graphs without understanding the dependency chain. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_71
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class Handler { public Handler(IClock clock) { } }
        interface IClock { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(Handler).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}

    Q1.72 How can container interview framing in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Container interview framing means strong answers connect the container to startup composition, lifetimes, and maintainability. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with terminology only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime behavior. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;

    Q1.73 What is built in DI container role in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Built in di container role means ASP.NET Core includes a built-in container that creates services and resolves dependency graphs at runtime. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual new chains everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring the container as the application composition mechanism. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_73
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderService, OrderService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IOrderService { }
        class OrderService : IOrderService { }
    }
}

    Q1.74 How does composition root concept in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Composition root concept means service wiring belongs in the startup composition root where application dependencies are declared intentionally. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with registration scattered through feature code, and they should avoid the trap of losing control of how the graph is assembled. Example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_74
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<IClock, SystemClock>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
    }
}

    Q1.75 Why does container versus object creation by hand in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Container versus object creation by hand means the container helps centralize creation rules lifetimes and replacement seams. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual creation in every layer, and they should avoid the trap of duplicating construction logic across the app. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_75
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new InvoiceService(new SystemClock());
        Console.WriteLine(service is not null);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
        class InvoiceService { public InvoiceService(IClock clock) { } }
    }
}

    public static void Run()
    {
        bool containerIsMagic = false;
        Console.WriteLine(containerIsMagic);
    }
}

    2. Service registration

    This section contains 100 interview questions focused on Service registration. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

    Q2.1 What problem does registration by contract and implementation in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Registration by contract and implementation means services are commonly registered against interfaces or concrete types to control how implementations are resolved. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with concrete new statements everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of registering services without design intent. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a service-registration review, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IEmailSender { }
        class SmtpEmailSender : IEmailSender { }
    }
}

    Q2.2 How would you explain registration style conventions in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Registration style conventions means consistent registration conventions improve readability and onboarding in larger apps. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with each file invents its own pattern, and they should avoid the trap of letting service setup become inconsistent noise. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        string convention = "AddFeatureServices";
        Console.WriteLine(convention);
    }
}

    Q2.3 Why is extension methods for service groups in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Extension methods for service groups means teams often group related registrations behind extension methods to keep Program.cs readable. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with one huge startup block, and they should avoid the trap of hiding registration logic without meaningful grouping. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IReportService, ReportService>();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IExportService, ExportService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IReportService { } interface IExportService { } class ReportService : IReportService { } class ExportService : IExportService { }
    }
}

    Q2.4 How can registration order awareness in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Registration order awareness means some registrations override or decorate earlier ones so order can matter in selected scenarios. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with order never matters, and they should avoid the trap of changing order blindly. Example: during a controller refactor, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool orderSensitive = true;
        Console.WriteLine(orderSensitive);
    }
}

    Q2.5 What is feature-based service modules in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Feature-based service modules means feature or layer-based registration can make service setup more maintainable as the app grows. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with all registrations in one undifferentiated block, and they should avoid the trap of letting composition root sprawl uncontrollably. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        string module = "OrdersModule";
        Console.WriteLine(module);
    }
}

    Q2.6 How does registration interview framing in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Registration interview framing means good answers connect registration shape to readability ownership and override behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with AddScoped syntax recall only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping why the pattern helps. Example: during a web request scope investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_6
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Registration style should stay readable as the app grows");
    }
}

    Q2.33 What problem does extension methods for service groups in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with one huge startup block, and they should avoid the trap of hiding registration logic without meaningful grouping. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_33
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IReportService, ReportService>();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IExportService, ExportService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IReportService { } interface IExportService { } class ReportService : IReportService { } class ExportService : IExportService { }
    }
}

    Q2.34 How would you explain registration order awareness in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Registration order awareness means some registrations override or decorate earlier ones so order can matter in selected scenarios. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with order never matters, and they should avoid the trap of changing order blindly. Example: during a controller refactor, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_34
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool orderSensitive = true;
        Console.WriteLine(orderSensitive);
    }
}

    Q2.35 Why is feature-based service modules in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Feature-based service modules means feature or layer-based registration can make service setup more maintainable as the app grows. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with all registrations in one undifferentiated block, and they should avoid the trap of letting composition root sprawl uncontrollably. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;

{

        Console.WriteLine(module);
    }
}

    Answer: Registration interview framing means good answers connect registration shape to readability ownership and override behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with AddScoped syntax recall only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping why the pattern helps. Example: during a web request scope investigation, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.     Q2.37 What is registration by contract and implementation in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Registration by contract and implementation means services are commonly registered against interfaces or concrete types to control how implementations are resolved. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with concrete new statements everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of registering services without design intent. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_37
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IEmailSender { }
        class SmtpEmailSender : IEmailSender { }
    }
}

    Q2.38 How does registration style conventions in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Registration style conventions means consistent registration conventions improve readability and onboarding in larger apps. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with each file invents its own pattern, and they should avoid the trap of letting service setup become inconsistent noise. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_38
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        string convention = "AddFeatureServices";
        Console.WriteLine(convention);
    }
}

Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a controller refactor, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_65
{

    {
        string module = "OrdersModule";
        Console.WriteLine(module);
    }
}

    Q2.67 Why is registration by contract and implementation in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Registration by contract and implementation means services are commonly registered against interfaces or concrete types to control how implementations are resolved. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with concrete new statements everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of registering services without design intent. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_67
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IEmailSender { }
        class SmtpEmailSender : IEmailSender { }
    }
}

    Q2.68 How can registration style conventions in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Registration style conventions means consistent registration conventions improve readability and onboarding in larger apps. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with each file invents its own pattern, and they should avoid the trap of letting service setup become inconsistent noise. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_68
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        string convention = "AddFeatureServices";
        Console.WriteLine(convention);
    }
}

    Q2.69 What is extension methods for service groups in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Extension methods for service groups means teams often group related registrations behind extension methods to keep Program.cs readable. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with one huge startup block, and they should avoid the trap of hiding registration logic without meaningful grouping. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_69
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IReportService, ReportService>();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IExportService, ExportService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IReportService { } interface IExportService { } class ReportService : IReportService { } class ExportService : IExportService { }
    }
}

    Q2.70 How does registration order awareness in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Registration order awareness means some registrations override or decorate earlier ones so order can matter in selected scenarios. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with order never matters, and they should avoid the trap of changing order blindly. Example: during a service-registration review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so maintenance risk stays lower.

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

{

        Console.WriteLine(orderSensitive);
    }

        builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IEmailSender { }
        class SmtpEmailSender : IEmailSender { }
    }
}

    Q2.98 How would you explain registration style conventions in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Registration style conventions means consistent registration conventions improve readability and onboarding in larger apps. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with each file invents its own pattern, and they should avoid the trap of letting service setup become inconsistent noise. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_98
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        string convention = "AddFeatureServices";
        Console.WriteLine(convention);
    }
}

    Q2.99 Why is extension methods for service groups in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Extension methods for service groups means teams often group related registrations behind extension methods to keep Program.cs readable. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with one huge startup block, and they should avoid the trap of hiding registration logic without meaningful grouping. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_99
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IReportService, ReportService>();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IExportService, ExportService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IReportService { } interface IExportService { } class ReportService : IReportService { } class ExportService : IExportService { }
    }
}

    Q2.100 How can registration order awareness in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Registration order awareness means some registrations override or decorate earlier ones so order can matter in selected scenarios. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with order never matters, and they should avoid the trap of changing order blindly. Example: during a service-registration review, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_100
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool orderSensitive = true;
        Console.WriteLine(orderSensitive);
    }
}

    3. Service lifetimes

    This section contains 100 interview questions focused on Service lifetimes. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

    Q3.1 What is singleton scoped transient basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Singleton scoped transient basics means service lifetime controls reuse, scope, and disposal behavior across the app. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with all services can share one lifetime, and they should avoid the trap of choosing lifetimes by habit. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<SingletonService>();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<ScopedService>();
        builder.Services.AddTransient<TransientService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class SingletonService { } class ScopedService { } class TransientService { }
    }
}

    Q3.2 How does singleton trade-offs in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Singleton trade-offs means singletons are reused broadly and must be thread-safe and dependency-safe for long application lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with singleton everything for performance, and they should avoid the trap of putting request-specific assumptions into singletons. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool singletonNeedsThreadSafety = true;
        Console.WriteLine(singletonNeedsThreadSafety);
    }
}

    Q3.3 Why does scoped lifetime behavior in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Scoped lifetime behavior means scoped services typically live for a request or explicit scope and fit request-bound operations. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with singleton assumptions for request data, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting scope boundaries. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool requestBound = true;
        Console.WriteLine(requestBound);
    }
}

    Q3.4 When should you use transient lifetime behavior in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Transient lifetime behavior means transients create a new instance per resolution and fit stateless or lightweight services. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with reuse assumptions for transients, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring creation cost and disposal expectations. Example: during a controller refactor, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool lightweight = true;
        Console.WriteLine(lightweight);
    }
}

    Q3.5 What problem does capturing scoped in singleton bug in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Capturing scoped in singleton bug means injecting shorter-lived services into longer-lived services can create invalid object graphs. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with lifetime mismatch is harmless, and they should avoid the trap of allowing scoped services to leak into singletons. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a controller refactor, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<BadSingleton>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class BadSingleton { }
    }
}

    Answer: Lifetime interview framing means strong answers connect lifetimes to ownership reuse and thread-safety trade-offs. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with three lifetime names only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime consequences. Example: during a service-registration review, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_30
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Lifetime choice affects reuse, disposal, and safety");
    }
}

    Q3.31 Why is singleton scoped transient basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Singleton scoped transient basics means service lifetime controls reuse, scope, and disposal behavior across the app. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with all services can share one lifetime, and they should avoid the trap of choosing lifetimes by habit. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_31
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<SingletonService>();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<ScopedService>();
        builder.Services.AddTransient<TransientService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class SingletonService { } class ScopedService { } class TransientService { }
    }
}

    Q3.32 How can singleton trade-offs in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Singleton trade-offs means singletons are reused broadly and must be thread-safe and dependency-safe for long application lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with singleton everything for performance, and they should avoid the trap of putting request-specific assumptions into singletons. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_32
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool singletonNeedsThreadSafety = true;
        Console.WriteLine(singletonNeedsThreadSafety);
    }
}

    Q3.33 What is scoped lifetime behavior in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Scoped lifetime behavior means scoped services typically live for a request or explicit scope and fit request-bound operations. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with singleton assumptions for request data, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting scope boundaries. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_33
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool requestBound = true;
        Console.WriteLine(requestBound);
    }
}

    Q3.34 How does transient lifetime behavior in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Transient lifetime behavior means transients create a new instance per resolution and fit stateless or lightweight services. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with reuse assumptions for transients, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring creation cost and disposal expectations. Example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_34
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool lightweight = true;
        Console.WriteLine(lightweight);
    }
}

    Q3.35 Why does capturing scoped in singleton bug in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Capturing scoped in singleton bug means injecting shorter-lived services into longer-lived services can create invalid object graphs. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with lifetime mismatch is harmless, and they should avoid the trap of allowing scoped services to leak into singletons. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_35
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<BadSingleton>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class BadSingleton { }
    }
}

using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool singletonNeedsThreadSafety = true;
        Console.WriteLine(singletonNeedsThreadSafety);
    }
}

    Q3.63 Why is scoped lifetime behavior in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Scoped lifetime behavior means scoped services typically live for a request or explicit scope and fit request-bound operations. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with singleton assumptions for request data, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting scope boundaries. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_63
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool requestBound = true;
        Console.WriteLine(requestBound);
    }
}

    Q3.64 How can transient lifetime behavior in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Transient lifetime behavior means transients create a new instance per resolution and fit stateless or lightweight services. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with reuse assumptions for transients, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring creation cost and disposal expectations. Example: during a controller refactor, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_64
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool lightweight = true;
        Console.WriteLine(lightweight);
    }
}

    Q3.65 What is capturing scoped in singleton bug in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Capturing scoped in singleton bug means injecting shorter-lived services into longer-lived services can create invalid object graphs. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with lifetime mismatch is harmless, and they should avoid the trap of allowing scoped services to leak into singletons. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_65
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<BadSingleton>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class BadSingleton { }
    }
}

    Q3.66 How does lifetime interview framing in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Lifetime interview framing means strong answers connect lifetimes to ownership reuse and thread-safety trade-offs. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with three lifetime names only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime consequences. Example: during a web request scope investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_66
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Lifetime choice affects reuse, disposal, and safety");
    }
}

    Q3.67 Why does singleton scoped transient basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Singleton scoped transient basics means service lifetime controls reuse, scope, and disposal behavior across the app. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with all services can share one lifetime, and they should avoid the trap of choosing lifetimes by habit. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_67
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<SingletonService>();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<ScopedService>();
        builder.Services.AddTransient<TransientService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class SingletonService { } class ScopedService { } class TransientService { }
    }
}

    Q3.68 When should you use singleton trade-offs in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Singleton trade-offs means singletons are reused broadly and must be thread-safe and dependency-safe for long application lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with singleton everything for performance, and they should avoid the trap of putting request-specific assumptions into singletons. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

{

        Console.WriteLine(singletonNeedsThreadSafety);
    }
}

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_95
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<BadSingleton>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class BadSingleton { }
    }
}

    Q3.96 How can lifetime interview framing in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Lifetime interview framing means strong answers connect lifetimes to ownership reuse and thread-safety trade-offs. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with three lifetime names only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime consequences. Example: during a web request scope investigation, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_96
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Lifetime choice affects reuse, disposal, and safety");
    }
}

    Q3.97 What is singleton scoped transient basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Singleton scoped transient basics means service lifetime controls reuse, scope, and disposal behavior across the app. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with all services can share one lifetime, and they should avoid the trap of choosing lifetimes by habit. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_97
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<SingletonService>();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<ScopedService>();
        builder.Services.AddTransient<TransientService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class SingletonService { } class ScopedService { } class TransientService { }
    }
}

    Q3.98 How does singleton trade-offs in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Singleton trade-offs means singletons are reused broadly and must be thread-safe and dependency-safe for long application lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with singleton everything for performance, and they should avoid the trap of putting request-specific assumptions into singletons. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_98
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool singletonNeedsThreadSafety = true;
        Console.WriteLine(singletonNeedsThreadSafety);
    }
}

    Q3.99 Why does scoped lifetime behavior in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Scoped lifetime behavior means scoped services typically live for a request or explicit scope and fit request-bound operations. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with singleton assumptions for request data, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting scope boundaries. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_99
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool requestBound = true;
        Console.WriteLine(requestBound);
    }
}

    Q3.100 When should you use transient lifetime behavior in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Transient lifetime behavior means transients create a new instance per resolution and fit stateless or lightweight services. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with reuse assumptions for transients, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring creation cost and disposal expectations. Example: during a service-registration review, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_100
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool lightweight = true;
        Console.WriteLine(lightweight);
    }
}

    4. Constructor injection

    This section contains 100 interview questions focused on Constructor injection. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

    Q4.1 What problem does constructor injection basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Constructor injection basics means constructor injection makes dependencies explicit and required at object creation time. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with hidden property injection everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of creating objects with missing required collaborators. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a service-registration review, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class OrdersController { public OrdersController(IOrderService service) { Service = service; } public IOrderService Service { get; } }
        interface IOrderService { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(OrdersController).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}

    Q4.2 How would you explain required dependency clarity in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Required dependency clarity means constructor parameters show what a type truly needs to do its work. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with ambient dependencies only, and they should avoid the trap of hiding important collaborators behind globals or locators. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class InvoiceService { public InvoiceService(IClock clock, ILogger logger) { } }
        interface IClock { } interface ILogger { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(InvoiceService).GetConstructors().Single().GetParameters().Length);
    }
}

    Q4.3 Why is constructor size and design smell in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Constructor size and design smell means too many constructor dependencies can reveal that a type has too many responsibilities. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with big constructors are always fine, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring cohesion signals. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class LargeService { public LargeService(IA a, IB b, IC c, ID d) { } }
        interface IA { } interface IB { } interface IC { } interface ID { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(LargeService).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}

    Q4.4 How can optional dependencies and alternatives in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Optional dependencies and alternatives means optional behavior often needs better design than a long list of nullable constructor arguments. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with nullable dependency sprawl, and they should avoid the trap of forcing constructor signatures to model every variation poorly. Example: during a controller refactor, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool optionalDependency = false;
        Console.WriteLine(optionalDependency);
    }
}

    Q4.5 What is testability benefits of constructor injection in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

    Answer: Testability benefits of constructor injection means constructor injection makes substitution easier in unit tests and composition easier in startup. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with manual mutation after construction, and they should avoid the trap of making tests depend on hidden state changes. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var sut = new BillingService(new FakeGateway());
        Console.WriteLine(sut is not null);
        interface IGateway { } class FakeGateway : IGateway { } class BillingService { public BillingService(IGateway gateway) { } }
    }
}

    Answer: Constructor size and design smell means too many constructor dependencies can reveal that a type has too many responsibilities. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with big constructors are always fine, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring cohesion signals. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the composition boundary stays clearer.     Answer: Constructor injection interview framing means good answers tie constructor injection to explicit design seams and maintainability.
